coredata.dc:title: Fully flexible dye-sensitized solar cells photoanode modified with titanium dioxide-graphene quantum dot light scattering layer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Mustafa, Muhammad Norhaffis
Sulaiman, Yusran
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report the preparation of titanium dioxide-graphene quantum dot (TG) as a light scattering layer (LSL) using facile electrodeposition and drop-casting. A fully flexible DSSC device containing TG LSL was capable to generate efficiency of 5.18% which is 95% higher than the bare photoanode (2.65%). Upon the inclusion of TG LSL, the vast enhancement of efficiency was observed which attributed to the improvement in dye loading capacity and light scattering ability. The fully flexible DSSC also depicted higher charge collection efficiency compared to the bare photoanode, indicating enhanced light scattering ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitized solar cell
Titanium dioxide-graphene quantum dot
Electrodeposition
Light scattering layer
Fully flexible DSSC device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD diffractograms of bare photoanode, T LSL, G LSL and TG LSL. The peaks labeled with “♥” are corresponded to the peaks of GQD while the peaks labeled with “♠”, “♣” and “♦” are ascribed to the (A) anatase, (R) rutile and (B) brookite phase of TiO2, respectively.
FESEM images of (a) bare photoanode, (b) bare photoanode + G LSL, (c) bare photoanode + T LSL and (d) bare photoanode + TG LSL.
EDX spectrum of (a) bare photoanode, (b) bare photoanode + G LSL, (c) bare photoanode + T LSL and (d) bare photoanode + TG LSL.

(a) Nyquist plots (inset is the zoomed-in Nyquist plots of bare photoanode and bare photoanode + G LSL), (b) bode plots and (c) EIS equivalent circuit of bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) diffuse reflectance spectra of bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL.
(a) Dark J-V curve and (b) illuminated J-V curve of bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL.
Atomic (%) of each element present in the photoanode.
Electrochemical data of bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL.
Comparison and photovoltaic performances of flexible DSSCs.

head.ce:title: Fully flexible dye-sensitized solar cells photoanode modified with titanium dioxide-graphene quantum dot light scattering layer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Devices Laboratory, Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Devices Laboratory, Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report the preparation of titanium dioxide-graphene quantum dot (TG) as a light scattering layer (LSL) using facile electrodeposition and drop-casting. A fully flexible DSSC device containing TG LSL was capable to generate efficiency of 5.18% which is 95% higher than the bare photoanode (2.65%). Upon the inclusion of TG LSL, the vast enhancement of efficiency was observed which attributed to the improvement in dye loading capacity and light scattering ability. The fully flexible DSSC also depicted higher charge collection efficiency compared to the bare photoanode, indicating enhanced light scattering ability.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays the world's energy demand keeps rising due to the increment in the world total population. According to the United Nation, the world’s population is predicted to increase up to 10.9 billion by 2100 compared to the world’s population in 2019 (7.7 billion). The increment in the world’s population growth also triggered the increase in the world's energy demand, resulting in more usage of non-renewable energy such as fossil fuel. Therefore, in order to preserve energy security and prevent environmental problems such as the greenhouse effect and air pollution caused by fossil fuel, our attention should be shifted to green energy sources. One of the green energy alternatives is solar energy and among them, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have gathered great courtesy attributable to the remarkable properties including easy fabrication process, inexpensive and able to generate moderate power conversion efficiency (PCE). However, some improvements can be employed in the photoanode to increase the PCE. The addition of a light scattering layer (LSL) can enhance the light scattering ability by trapping some of the incident light. The extra incident light can trigger more excitation of electrons of the dye molecules, causing more voltage and photocurrent to be generated. According to Mie theory, the LSL should have a larger size than the photoanodes in the size range of 250–300 nm to increase the light-trapping ability (Gong et al., 2017; Steenfos et al., 1998; Szymanski et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2012a). Metal oxides are the most likely materials to be used as LSLs. Among the metal oxides, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most frequently used material as an LSL due to its remarkable properties such as abundant (Jiwei Ma et al., 2017), conductive (Mardare et al., 2002), high porosity (Tiainen et al., 2010), high photocatalytic activity (Ramesh et al., 2016) and facile fabrication process. Yu et al. (2019) have prepared TiO2 nanorod (NR) as LSL and the PCE increased up to 3.47% due to the improvement of photocurrent and enhancement in dye loading capacity. Zhang et al. (2019) have synthesized TiO2 nanowire with nanoscale whiskers as LSL via a simple one-step hydrothermal process. The results showed a promising increase in the efficiency of up to 5.98% compared to the bare photoanode (5.34%), signifying improvement in light-harvesting efficiency.

Other than metal oxides, carbon-based materials such as graphene (Khedr et al., 2019), graphene quantum dot (GQD) (Kim et al., 2019), reduced graphene oxide (rGO) (Banik et al., 2019) and multiwalled-carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) (Parvazian et al., 2014) can also be considered as potential candidates as light scattering materials in DSSCs. Among the carbon-based materials, GQD is the most suitable candidate as LSL due to the photoluminescence effect of GQD that capable to increase the range of light-harvesting from visible light to ultraviolet light. Kim et al. (2019) have reported that the addition of GQDs as LSL was successfully enhanced the DSSC performance up to 6.17% which was 45% higher than the photoanode without LSL as a result of the increase in the quantum efficiency. In another work demonstrated by Kumar et al. (2019) has shown that photoanode with GQD LSL fabricated on indium tin oxide/polyethylene naphthalate (ITO/PEN) substrate via the doctor blade technique depicted a PCE enhancement of 4.4% compared to the bare photoanode (4.2%).

A lot of researchers have paid more attention to the fabrication of flexible DSSC devices due to lightweight, attractive design and an increase in the public interest. Instead of rigid glass as a substrate in DSSC devices, flexible substrates such as titanium foil, indium tin oxide coated polyethylene terephthalate (ITO/PET) and ITO/PEN become more popular not only due to their remarkable traits but also can produce moderate PCE. Dongshe et al. (2004) reported the fabrication of porous nanocrystalline TiO2 via a hydrothermal method on a flexible ITO/PET substrate and a PCE of 2.5% was successfully generated. Kishore Kumar et al. (2019) fabricated a flexible DSSC device using a doctor blade technique on ITO/PEN substrate. The work was mainly focused on varying the stirring time and the TiO2 solution stirred at 8 h produced the highest PCE of 4.2% due to the absence in the formation of a crack in morphology compared to other stirring times. The longer stirring time led to the formation of agglomerated particles and reduced the surface area, resulting in low PCE (Kishore Kumar et al., 2019). Baiju et al. (2020) proposed a new technique to prepare a flexible photoanode via heat sink assisted elevated temperature sintering. The flexible photoanode sintered at 250 °C was capable to achieve a PCE of 4.11% without damaging the flexible ITO/PET substrate.

In this study, we highlighted the fabrication of fully flexible DSSC devices on the flexible ITO/PEN substrate consisting of TG LSL via a facile electrodeposition technique and drop-casting method. The photoanode with TG LSL displayed high dye loading capacity, high reflectance, high charge collection efficiency, resulting in an increase in the PCE up to 5.18%.

The redox electrolyte, Iodolyte Z-100 and the sensitizer N719) were supplied by Solaronix SA. The GQD was purchased from ACS materials. Acetonitrile and tert-butanol were obtained from Friendemann Schmidt and Bendosen, respectively. Titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP), TiO2 (Degussa, P25), chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate and lithium perchlorate (LiClO4) were procured Sigma Aldrich. Ethanol, acetone and sodium hydroxide were purchased from J.Kollin Chemicals, QReC® and Friendemann Schmidt, respectively. Nitric acid and potassium chloride were provided by Fisher Scientific. ITO/PEN (15 O/sq) was obtained from Peccel Japan. Deionized water (Millipore Milli-Q, 18.3 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experimental works.

The paste of TiO2 was formed by stirring 0.2 g TiO2 nanopowder, 0.2 mL nitric acid, 1.4 mL tert-butanol and 0.5 mL distilled water. The TiO2 paste was deposited onto the ITO/PEN substrate using a doctor blade technique and was heated at 190 °C for 2 h. The as-prepared photoanode was denoted as bare photoanode. The TiO2 LSL was then prepared by facile electrodeposition from an aqueous solution of 0.1 M TTIP in 0.1 M LiClO4. The potential range applied for the cyclic voltammetry (CV) was 0 to −1.5 V at a scan rate of 0.025 V/s and the CV was scanned for 2 cycles. This photoanode was denoted as photoanode + T LSL. Photoanode + G LSL and photoanode + TG LSL were prepared by drop-casting 5 μL solution containing 1 mg/mL GQD onto bare photoanode and photoanode + T LSL. A dye bath solution containing 0.2 mM N719 solution dissolved in the same ratio of tert-butanol and acetonitrile was used to immerse all the photoanodes for 24 h. The platinum counter electrode was prepared in an aqueous solution containing 0.2 mM chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate and 0.1 M LiClO4. The solution was electrodeposited potentiostatically at −0.6 V for 300 s using a three-electrode system. The ITO/PEN, platinum wire and silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) were used as working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The sensitized photoanodes were sandwiched together with the platinum counter electrode and the redox electrolyte (Iodolyte Z-100) was injected in between both electrodes.

The structural analysis and phase composition of the as-prepared photoanodes were studied by Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Shimadzu spectrometer) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu diffractometer) with Cu Kα as the X-ray source (λ = 1.54 Å), respectively. The surface morphologies and atomic percentage of the as-prepared photoanodes were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7600F) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The particle size of the as-prepared samples was measured using Image J software. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) study was examined using a two-electrode device assembly with the presence of redox electrolyte. Meanwhile, the open circuit potential, alternating current amplitude and frequency range were set at 0.8 V, 5 mV and (100 kHz − 1 Hz), respectively under dark condition. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) analysis and reflectance analyses were performed using Autolab Spectrophotometer UA. The former was performed to observe the changes in dye loading capacity upon addition of LSL by desorbing the sensitized photoanode in the solution containing 0.1 M sodium hydroxide while, the latter was executed to investigate the light scattering ability of the as-prepared photoanode. The photovoltaic analyses were conducted using a solar simulator (Oriel LCS-100) under standard one sun illumination. A NOVA software-equipped potentiostat (Autolab PGSTAT202) was used to perform both electrochemical and photovoltaic analyses.

The identification of functional groups and the crystallinity of LSL were examined by FTIR and XRD measurements. Fig. 1 a displays the FTIR spectra of all as-prepared LSL and bare photoanode. The FTIR spectra of bare photoanode and T LSL depict the same bands at 785, 1682 and 2941 cm−1 which are ascribed to the Ti-O-Ti stretching, O-H bending vibration, and O-H stretching of TiO2, respectively (Ali et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2012; Mustafa et al., 2019). The FTIR spectrum of G LSL shows the bands at 1475, 1703 and 2941 cm−1 which are ascribed to the O-H stretching, C = O stretching and C = C stretching of GQD, respectively (Dinari et al., 2016; Syed Zainol Abidin et al., 2017). The FTIR spectrum of TG LSL spectacles a combination of bands presence in the TiO2 and GQD spectra. Fig. 1b shows the XRD diffractograms of bare photoanode, T LSL, G LSL and TG LSL. The XRD pattern of bare photoanode depicts characteristic peaks at 25.6° A(101), 27.7° R(110), 36.2° R(101), 37.9° R(200), 41.3° R(111), 48.2° A(200), 54.1° R(211) and 55.3° R(220) which are ascribed to the anatase and rutile phase of TiO2 (JCPDS 01-073-1764). The XRD pattern of G LSL displays a characteristic peak at 23.4° indexed by (002) which is ascribed to the GQD (Huang et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017). The T LSL reveals the same peaks as bare photoanode with additional peaks at 31.1° B(121), 51.2° A(105) and 58.2° B(123) which are attributed to the anatase, rutile and brookite phase of TiO2 (JCPDS file No-29-1360). Meanwhile, the XRD pattern of TG LSL displays the same characteristic peaks as the combination of both T LSL and G LSL XRD patterns.

Fig. 2 a displays uniform porous spherical nanoparticles of TiO2 (43 ± 4 nm). As shown in Fig. 2b, the inclusion of GQD as LSL does not change the morphology of the bare photoanode. The introduction of electrodeposited T LSL shows a cluster of spherical nanoparticles of TiO2 (258 ± 26 nm), which is important to reflect the incident light into the sensitized T, resulting in the improvement of light scattering effect (Fig. 2c) (Usami, 1997). A combination of both electrodeposited TiO2 and GQD as LSL produces network-like structures that cover the surface of the bare photoanode (Fig. 2d). These network-like structures are capable to increase light scattering effect by trapping the incident light, resulting in more excitation of electrons and subsequently can increase the PCE. The EDX analysis was performed to ratify the presence of the constituent elements of TiO2 and GQD. Fig. 3 a and 3c depict all the elements attributed to the TiO2 while Fig. 3b and 3d display a combination of elements present in the TiO2 and GQD. The presence of the carbon element in the EDX spectra of Fig. 3b and 3d implies that the GQD was successfully drop-casted onto the photoanode. The summary of the atomic percentage of each constituent element of TiO2 and GQD is listed in Table 1 .

Fig. 4 a presents the Nyquist plots of bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL. The intersection of the high-frequency semicircle at the real axis implies the series resistance (R s) while, R ct1 (semicircle at a higher frequency) and R ct2 (semicircle at a lower frequency) represent the charge transfer resistance at the counter electrode/electrolyte interface and TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface, respectively (Lin et al., 2011). As shown in Table 2 , the R s value increased in the order of bare photoanode < bare photoanode + G LSL < bare photoanode + T LSL < bare photoanode + TG LSL which indicates that the addition of LSL on top of the photoanode increases the internal resistant of the DSSCs (Deepak et al., 2014). At the same time, the trend of both R ct1 and R ct2 also increases in the same order as R s, demonstrating the escalation in the overall resistance of the DSSC due to the additional layer on top of the photoanode (Zhang et al., 2012b). Fig. 4b displays the Bode plots of bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL. The maximum frequencies (f max) of all the photoanodes with the LSL are shifted to the lower frequency regions compared to the bare photoanode. The f max is inversely proportional to the electron lifetime (τn) (Eq. (1)), therefore the decrease in f max produces a longer τn.
(1)
τ n
=
1
2 π
f
max

The summary of the electrochemical data is listed in Table 2. The charge collection efficiency (η c) can be calculated using Eq. (2) and the η c is directly affected by the value of R ct2. The bare photoanode + TG LSL displays the highest R ct2 and η c, resulting in an enhanced light scattering effect and produces more photocurrent for high PCE.
(2)
η c
= ( 1 +
R s
R
c t 2
)
- 1

Fig. 4c illustrates an equivalent circuit that was assembled by fitting all the Nyquist plots. The circuit consists of R s, R ct1, R ct2 and constant phase element (CPE), where the CPE shows the inhomogeneity of photoanode after being modified (Azman et al., 2018). All the photoanodes depict low chi-square (x2 ) approximately less than 0.0001, indicating the equivalent circuit fits well the Nyquist plots.

UV–Vis analyses were carried out to study the LSL effect on the dye adsorption of the photoanode. As shown in Fig. 5 a, bare photoanode + TG LSL depicts the highest absorption peaks, demonstrating increases in the dye loading capacity of the photoanode. The concentrations of the dye molecules adsorbed by the bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL are 0.126, 0.144, 0.152 and 0.182 mmol dm−3, respectively. Upon the inclusion of LSLs on top of the photoanode, the amount of dye molecules adsorbed also increases, signifying an upsurge in the surface area for enhancing high dye loading capacity (Chen et al., 2013). The bare photoanode with TG LSL shows the highest concentration of the dye molecules adsorbed compared to the bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL and bare photoanode + T LSL because of the synergistic effect of the high surface area of both TiO2 and GQD (Zhang et al., 2018) which lead to the enhancement of the dye loading capacity. The light-scattering properties of the photoanode with and without LSL were examined using diffuse reflectance analysis as illustrated in Fig. 5b. The reflectance increases in the following order bare photoanode < bare photoanode + G LSL < bare photoanode + T LSL < bare photoanode + TG LSL, implying the addition of LSL enhancing the light scattering effect. The bare photoanode + TG LSL reveals the highest reflectance compared to other photoanodes owing to the unique structure of TG LSL that covers the surface of the bare photoanode, ensuing in more trapping of the incident light and subsequently leads to more generation of photocurrent. In addition, upon the introduction of TG as LSL on top of the photoanode, the reflectance shows the highest compared to the other photoanodes due to the bigger particle size of TG LSL (Fig. 2d) (Zhang et al., 2010). This indicates that the bare photoanode with TG LSL possesses a superior light scattering effect which subsequently enhances the DSSC performance.

The dark current analysis was carried out to examine the back electron transfer in DSSCs. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the onset of the dark current of bare photoanode occurs at around +0.5 V, while the onset of the dark currents of bare photoanode +G LSL, bare photoanode +T LSL and bare photoanode +TG LSL occur at 0.55, 0.70 and 0.71 V, respectively. In addition, the dark current of the bare photoanode + TG LSL increases slowly compared to bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL, demonstrating the addition of TG as LSL is capable to suppress the back electron transfer and reduces the current leakage (Mustafa et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2015). Fig. 6b portrays the comparison of J-V curves of bare photoanode, bare photoanode + G LSL, bare photoanode + T LSL and bare photoanode + TG LSL under the illumination of light. Upon the addition of the LSLs, the short circuit current density (J sc) and open circuit voltage (V oc) increase compared to the bare photoanode, demonstrating enhanced light scattering ability. The LSLs can trap more incident light which increases the excitation of the electrons. As a result, more electron-hole-junction will be produced which leads to more production of photocurrent and photovoltage. The bare photoanode + TG LSL displays the highest fill factor (FF) compared to the other photoanodes, resulting in high DSSC performance.

As shown in Table 3 , the bare photoanode + TG LSL displays the highest PCE (5.18%) compared to the previous results in the literature which is attributed to the unique morphology of TG LSL that covers the bare photoanode. Thus, it increases the trapping of incident light that leads to an upsurge in the excitation of electrons, subsequently give rise to the J sc. In addition, the enhancement of the PCE is due to the synergistic effect of high surface area TiO2 (Ouzzine et al., 2014) that leads to the increment of dye loading capacity and the photoluminescent effect of GQD (Song et al., 2016) that contributes to the broadening of the range of light-harvesting from visible light to ultraviolet range, resulting in high DSSC performance. The V oc of the bare photoanode + TG LSL is comparable to the reported literature (Table 3). However, the FF depicted by the bare photoanode + TG LSL is slightly lower (Table 3) due to the increase in series resistance and charge transfer resistance at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface upon addition of TG LSL (Fig. 4a).

In a nutshell, we have successfully developed a fully flexible TG as LSL via the facile electrodeposition and drop-casting. The FTIR and XRD analyses confirmed the formation of each LSL on top of the bare photoanode whereas, the EDX analysis ratify the presence of the constituent elements of TiO2 and GQD in the photoanode. The TG LSL revealed a network-like structure that covers the surface of bare photoanode which not only enhanced the light scattering effect but also increased the dye loading capacity. The PCE of bare photoanode + TG LSL was successfully compared with bare photoanode + T LSL, bare photoanode + G LSL and bare photoanode. The electrochemical analyses showed improvement in the τn and η c upon the addition of TG LSL onto the photoanode. The bare photoanode + TG LSL displayed an enhancement of PCE up to 5.18% owing to a good synergistic effect and high light scattering ability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors were grateful to the Universiti Putra Malaysia for the financial support under Research Grant (UPM/800-3/3/1/GPB/2018/9659200).
